The **2000 Football League Trophy Final** (known as the **[Auto Windscreens](/wiki/Auto_Windscreens "Auto Windscreens") Shield** for sponsorship reasons) was the 17th final of the domestic football cup competition for teams from the [Second](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") and [Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") of the [Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League"). The match was played at [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)") on 16 April 2000, and was the last Football League Trophy final to be played there before the stadium closed for redevelopment. The match was contested by [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") and [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C."). The match was won by Stoke City, with [Graham Kavanagh](/wiki/Graham_Kavanagh "Graham Kavanagh") and [Peter Thorne](/wiki/Peter_Thorne_(English_footballer) "Peter Thorne (English footballer)") scoring in the 2–1 victory.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel-2)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2000_Football_League_Trophy_final&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

The [1999–2000 season](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Stoke_City_F.C._season "1999–2000 Stoke City F.C. season") saw both [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") and [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") involved in the race for promotion to the First Division. Stoke had been in the top six for most of the campaign whilst Bristol battled with city rivals [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers_F.C. "Bristol Rovers F.C.") to gain a play-off spot. The two sides had played each other twice before the final.[[2]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel-2) Firstly on 14 November 1999 at Stoke's [Britannia Stadium](/wiki/Britannia_Stadium "Britannia Stadium") the score ending 1–1 with goals from [Nicky Mohan](/wiki/Nicky_Mohan "Nicky Mohan") and [Brian Tinnion](/wiki/Brian_Tinnion "Brian Tinnion"). The second match at [Ashton Gate](/wiki/Ashton_Gate_Stadium "Ashton Gate Stadium") also ended in a draw this time 2–2 with goals from [Kyle Lightbourne](/wiki/Kyle_Lightbourne "Kyle Lightbourne"), [Graham Kavanagh](/wiki/Graham_Kavanagh "Graham Kavanagh") and a brace from [Tony Thorpe](/wiki/Tony_Thorpe "Tony Thorpe").[[2]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel-2)

## Route to the final

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2000_Football_League_Trophy_final&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Route to the final")]

Both sides had a fairly easy route to the final, Bristol City were handed a first round bye whilst Stoke over came [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington_F.C. "Darlington F.C.") 3–2 thanks to a Kyle Lightbourne golden goal. Bristol City beat [Cheltenham Town](/wiki/Cheltenham_Town_F.C. "Cheltenham Town F.C.") 3–1 in the second round and Stoke overcame [Oldham Athletic](/wiki/Oldham_Athletic_A.F.C. "Oldham Athletic A.F.C.") again via a golden goal.[[2]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel-2) In the quarters Bristol City eased past [Bournemouth](/wiki/A.F.C._Bournemouth "A.F.C. Bournemouth") on penalties as Stoke beat [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") 2–1. The semi-finals saw Bristol City cruise past [Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C.") 4–0 whilst Stoke needed a late goal from [James O'Connor](/wiki/James_O%27Connor_(footballer_born_1979) "James O'Connor (footballer born 1979)") to progress. In the area finals Bristol City beat [Exeter City](/wiki/Exeter_City_F.C. "Exeter City F.C.") 5–1 over two legs and Stoke won equality as easily beating [Rochdale](/wiki/Rochdale_A.F.C. "Rochdale A.F.C.") 4–1.[[2]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel-2)

## Match review

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2000_Football_League_Trophy_final&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Match review")]

Over 75,000 fans packed into [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)") to see Bristol City take on Stoke City in the [Football League Trophy](/wiki/Football_League_Trophy "Football League Trophy") final for which Stoke fans dedicated to [Stanley Matthews](/wiki/Stanley_Matthews "Stanley Matthews") who had died in February 2000.[[2]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel-2) Stoke made the bright start of the two sides enjoying a decent amount of possession and took the lead through [Graham Kavanagh](/wiki/Graham_Kavanagh "Graham Kavanagh") after 32 minutes after his shot beat the Robins 'keeper [Billy Mercer](/wiki/Billy_Mercer_(footballer_born_1969) "Billy Mercer (footballer born 1969)") at his near post. Leading 1–0 Stoke dropped deeper in the second half as they looked to cancel out the threat from the dangerous [Scott Murray](/wiki/Scott_Murray_(footballer_born_1974) "Scott Murray (footballer born 1974)") and [Tony Thorpe](/wiki/Tony_Thorpe "Tony Thorpe"). It looked to be working but with 15 minutes left [Paul Holland](/wiki/Paul_Holland_(footballer) "Paul Holland (footballer)") headed in a corner to make the scores 1–1.[[2]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel-2) However it was Stoke who had the final say, a counterattack by Stoke was halted when [Bjarni Guðjónsson](/wiki/Bjarni_Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3nsson "Bjarni Guðjónsson") was fouled by [Louis Carey](/wiki/Louis_Carey "Louis Carey") to conceded a free-kick. Whilst Bristol complied about the awarding of the free-kick, Guðjónsson played a quick pass to Kavanagh who crossed in to [Peter Thorne](/wiki/Peter_Thorne_(English_footballer) "Peter Thorne (English footballer)") to earn Stoke their third win at Wembley.[[2]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel-2)
